questions.question: The term "Peering" refers to: (1926)

The exchange of information between a router and a station using a routing protocol
The exchange of information between two OSPF routers connected by a virtual link
The exchange of information between two OSPF routers of the same area

questions.question: A Neutral Access Point is a particular network in which: (2166)
options: Several Autonomous Systems connect, at level 2, a certain number of routers in order to exchange routing information.
Several Autonomous Systems connect, at level 3, a certain number of routers in order to exchange routing information.
An Autonomous System connects, at level 2, a certain number of routers in order to speed up the exchange of routing information within the domain.
An Autonomous System connects, at level 3, a certain number of routers in order to speed up the exchange of routing information within the domain.

questions.question: Redistribution: (1924)
options: is the process that must be enabled on the router to ensure that it can sort packets to the appropriate destination
is used to exchange information between an internal router (interior gateway) and an external router (exterior gateway) that uses the BGP protocol
It is used above all by peripheral routing domains, which connect to a single Internet service provider for Internet access
is used to allow the passage of routing information from a routing domain A to a routing domain B

questions.question: redistribution in the context of routing protocols consists in (2146)
options: distributing the routing table across multiple devices in order to reduce memory occupation on each device
redistributing traffic across multiple alternative paths to fully exploit network resources and avoid congestion on specific paths
communicating via a routing protocol routes acquired with a other, even if this results in a loss of information
learn how to reach destinations without having to exchange routing information with other computers

questions.question: Inter-domain routing: (1923)
options: It requires that each router knows exactly the path, in terms of routers crossed, taken by packets towards a destination
It requires that an exterior gateway makes choices of paths, based on information collected through inter-domain routing protocols, consistent with existing agreements with other autonomous systems

It requires that each router knows exactly the cost of reaching any destination (for example in terms of bandwidth of links crossed) in order to calculate the path with the lowest cost (for example with the highest bandwidth)
is a concept that will tend to disappear

questions.question: Suppose the existence of three AS (Autonomous System) connected sequentially (A-B-C). If intermediate AS B wants to prevent its network from being used as a transit from A to C: (1928)
options: It must perform route masking to A
It must set up an access list ("packet filtering") at the entrance of its domain that discards all incoming packets from A to C
It must set up route masking to A and an access list at the entrance of its domain on packets from A to C
AS B cannot block traffic, since each AS must provide transit to its adjacent ASes

questions.question: To support variable-length network prefixes in a network area where dynamic routing is used: (168)
options: The netmask associated with the prefix must be provided to the routers during configuration (with the "network" command on Cisco routers)
The netmask is automatically deduced from the class of addresses used
The routing information exchanged between routers must include the subnet mask associated with each prefix advertised
Is it mandatory to use OSPF or BGP protocols

questions.question: An Autonomous System:
options: is identified by means of a 4 bytes long ID assigned by the CSA
is a set of subnets with a short topological proximity and managed by a single organization unit
is a subnet configured by leveraging the static routing
is identified by means of a 4 bytes long ID automatically computed by the BGP

questions.question: Multipath routing: (2175)
options: is supported by most RIP implementations
It allows routers to use multiple paths during network transients, so as to speed up the dissemination of routing information and decrease the duration of the transient
It can cause loops if it is admitted that the paths used may have different costs
It is recommended only if the data traffic is mainly composed of TCP packets

questions.question: The IS-IS protocol (1976)
options: It is an obsolete routing protocol no longer used due to its poor performance
It is a protocol routing based on the link state algorithm widely used in large networks
It is a protocol used by Ethernet switches to create a routing tree in the network (spanning tree) eliminating closed paths
It is an evolution of BGP protocol for the exchange of routing information between routers belonging to different autonomous systems

questions.question: The RIP protocol is enveloped: (2169)
options: Directly in IP, to limit the size of routing packets
Directly in IP, to allow broadcast transmission of packets
In UDP, to allow broadcast transmission of packets
In UDP, for reasons mainly related to greater simplicity of software development

questions.question: The RIP protocol provides mechanisms to reduce the possibility of loops occurring: (1930)
options: Through the analysis of packets in transit and the identification of those that pass more than once from the same router
By means of "traceroute" processes activated periodically
Through "Split-Horizon" and "Hold-Down" mechanisms
None of the above

questions.question: The RIP protocol is characterized by (1975)
options: The use of the link state routing algorithm
The possibility of being used for both interdomain routing and for routing intradomain
The ability to operate on large networks due to its ability to operate in a hierarchical way
Frequent instabilities and ease in creating circular forwarding paths

questions.question: The main limitation of the RIP routing protocol compared to IGRP is that: (1931)
options: Since RIP is proprietary, it is not available on all routers
The RIP metric is less indicative, compared to that of IGRP, of the real degree of preferability of a network path compared to others
It does not allow, unlike IGRP, hierarchical routing
it is a Distance Vector type protocol, therefore less scalable than IGRP (Link State)

questions.question: RIP, feature: (1946)
options: Requires administrator configuration to function
Communicates information with neighboring routers
Can detect IP addresses of connected hosts and communicate them to neighboring routers

questions.question: An OSPF Router Link LSA describes: (170)
options: A link that connects the advertising router with one of its neighboring routers
A summary of reachable networks
The list of connected routers in a LAN
The current value of parameters (CPU load, link load, uptime, etc.) related to a link between two routers

questions.question: The OSPF routing protocol chooses the path to a destination taking into account: (1933)
options: Length of each link along the path
Bandwidth and delay for each link
Can be configured to use various metrics whose semantics are established by the network manager
Hop Count

questions.question: In the OSPF protocol a regime all routers have in memory: (1936)
options: The same tree of optimal paths
The database describing the area to which they belong
The same database describing the entire AS
A set of Distance Vector of all adjacent routers

questions.question: A difference between the OSPF routing protocol and IGRP is that: (1932)
options: OSPF is hierarchical
OSPF also allows routing between different ASs
OSPF allows routing information relating to different protocol architectures to be transported at the same time (integrated routing)
OSPF is proprietary

questions.question: The BGP routing protocol: (1938)
options: Uses rules (policies) on additional information to cost metrics to identify the "best" to reach a destination
is used exclusively for information exchanges between routers of different autonomous systems
is used exclusively for information exchanges between routers of the same autonomous system
is the protocol that will replace OSPF

questions.question: In the BGP routing protocol: (19a0)
options: Topology information always takes precedence over the application of routing policies ("policies")
The application of routing policies ("policies") always takes precedence over topology information
The least-cost path to each destination is always chosen
The least-cost path to each destination is always chosen, unless there are limitations intrinsic to the operation of hierarchical routing

questions.question: What is the use of policing mechanisms? (I877)
options: They are used by the user to agree with the provider on the level of QoS to be obtained.
They are used by the service provider to verify that the traffic entered by the customer complies with the agreements made.
They are used by the user to verify that the traffic arriving from the provider complies with the agreements made.
They are used in the various routers to guarantee a maximum crossing time for each of them.

questions.question: DiffServ differs from IntServ because: (106)
options: DiffServ tends to provide a QoS guarantee that IntServ does not.
DiffServ introduces new protocols to allow the reservation of resources in order to obtain a given QoS.
IntServ tends to provide a QoS guarantee that DiffServ does not.
DiffServ tends to guarantee a maximum traversal time, while IntServ tends to provide a minimum guaranteed bandwidth.

questions.question: Using the token bucket algorithm (or holed bucket) with capacity B tokens and filling rate r tokens/s it is possible to control: (1836)
options: That the traversal time does not exceed rB seconds.
The number of packets per second injected does not exceed r, and the maximum burst does not exceed B.
The number of packets per second injected does not exceed B, and the maximum burst does not exceed r.
The jitter does not exceed B/r.

questions.question: The RSVP (Resource reSerVation Protocol) protocol is able to: (1371)
options: Limit delay variations (jitter) suffered by packets in routers
Let routers know the quality of service requests made by applications
Reserve computing resources in servers that share their processors
Check the delays and losses suffered by multimedia application packets on the network.

questions.question: Scheduling algorithms are used: (188%
options: In access routers, to ensure that the traffic generated by a user conforms to the traffic profile contracted with their service provider.
In firewalls, to delay packets that enter a corporate network from the Internet in order to prevent certain types of security attacks.
In routers, to decide what order the packets waiting at an interface should be transmitted.
In routers, to properly schedule the list of configuration commands given by the user in order to minimize the disruption caused by the time needed to apply the changes.

questions.question: The combination of token bucket (or holed bucket) and Weighted Fair Queueing (WFQ) mechanisms serves to guarantee: (1906)
options: A maximum router traversal time.
A maximum NAT traversal time.
A maximum bandwidth for each packet flow.
A maximum burst of consecutive packets, for each flow.

questions.question: In Diffserv, a "Class of Service" identifies: (191%)
options: A set of packets that belong to the same VoIP session
A set of packets that receive the same treatment by the router (for example, all VoIP traffic)
An operating mode of the edge routers, which must classify and mark incoming packets

questions.question: Quality of service can be guaranteed on packet networks when (66)
options: Packets are transported by a cell-switched infrastructure (e.g. ATM)
Network nodes implement appropriate mechanisms that regulate packet service (e.g. scheduling algorithms)
Applications are able to encode the information to be transferred according to levels (layers) of different importance

questions.question: In DiffServ, packet classification is usually done at:
options: Network edges.
Core routers only.
End hosts only.
Layer 2 switches only.

questions.question: The DSCP field is located in:
options: The IP header.
The TCP header.
The Ethernet header.
The application payload.

questions.question: Traffic shaping differs from policing because shaping:
options: Delays packets instead of dropping them immediately.
Drops packets immediately.
Encrypts packets.
Removes headers.

questions.question: A token bucket allows short bursts because:
options: Tokens can accumulate over time.
Packets are fragmented.
Routers buffer indefinitely.
Scheduling is always FIFO.

questions.question: According to NIST, cloud computing provides:
options: On-demand network access to shared configurable resources.
Dedicated hardware for every tenant.
Offline execution without networks.
Manual provisioning only.

questions.question: A key characteristic of cloud computing is:
options: On-demand self-service.
Fixed capacity per customer.
No virtualization.
No resource pooling.

questions.question: Resource pooling means:
options: Resources are shared among multiple tenants.
Each user owns the hardware.
Only one VM per server is allowed.
No monitoring is needed.

questions.question: Broad network access implies:
options: Services are available over the network via standard mechanisms.
Only local console access is possible.
Access requires a dedicated serial line.
Only administrators can connect.

questions.question: Measured service in cloud environments means:
options: Usage is monitored and can be billed.
Resources are unlimited and free.
Billing is always a fixed flat rate.
No metrics are collected.

questions.question: Elasticity refers to:
options: Dynamic scaling up/down with demand.
Replacing servers once per year.
Only adding bigger CPUs.
Static allocation of VMs.

questions.question: Pay-per-use is beneficial because:
options: Costs follow actual consumption.
It guarantees zero downtime.

It disables multi-tenancy.

questions.question: CapEx vs OpEx: cloud adoption typically shifts costs toward:
options: Operational expenditure (OpEx).
Capital expenditure (CapEx) only.
No cost model changes.

questions.question: Capacity planning primarily aims to:
options: Reduce mismatch between demand and available capacity.
Maximize over-provisioning permanently.
Eliminate all peaks forever.
Avoid measuring traffic.

questions.question: In a Lag strategy, capacity is added:
options: After demand exceeds current capacity.
Before demand increases.
At random times.
Only when hardware is replaced.

questions.question: In a Lead strategy, capacity is added:
options: In anticipation of demand growth.
Only after service disruption.
Only when costs drop.
Only at end of fiscal year.

questions.question: Match strategy in capacity planning tries to:
options: Add capacity in small increments to follow demand.
Always stay below demand.
Always stay far above demand.
Avoid monitoring demand.

questions.question: Vertical scaling means:
options: Replacing a resource with a more powerful one.
Adding more identical instances.
Splitting a VM into many pods.
Reducing network hops.

questions.question: Horizontal scaling means:
options: Adding/removing identical resources.
Upgrading one server CPU.
Replacing RAM with faster RAM only.
Disabling redundancy.

questions.question: Vertical scaling is less common in clouds mainly because:
options: It may require downtime for replacement.
It is always cheaper than horizontal scaling.
It needs no maintenance.
It cannot increase capacity.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the "southbound interface" in SDN?
options: To connect applications to the controller
To communicate between the controller and network devices
To manage the network's northbound traffic
To enable user interaction with the network

questions.question: What advantage does the "big switch" abstraction provide in SDN?
options: Hides the network's internal complexity
Integrates routing and switching into one device
Simplifies network hardware deployment
Eliminates the need for a controller

questions.question: What is "context-based forwarding" in SDN?
options: Pre-configured routing rules
Routing decisions based on real-time traffic patterns
Combining proactive and reactive configurations
Matching headers to predetermined rules

questions.question: In SDN, what is the main purpose of the "match-action" paradigm?
options: To log network traffic
To process packets based on matching rules and perform actions like forwarding or dropping
To encapsulate packets for multi-protocol routing
To configure the data plane's telemetry system

questions.question: What does the CAP theorem state in distributed systems?
options: Consistency, availability, and partition tolerance cannot all be achieved simultaneously
Control, action, and partitioning are always achievable
Centralized systems are more efficient than distributed ones
OpenFlow and SDN are required for achieving scalability

questions.question: What does a "Service Function Chain (SFC)" represent in NFV?
options: A set of virtual machines hosting applications
A chain of network functions applied to traffic in sequence
A routing protocol optimized for cloud networks
A switch-level abstraction for dynamic forwarding

questions.question: What is the purpose of the "northbound interface" in SDN?
options: Connects the controller to the applications
Communicates between switches and controllers
Provides a GUI for network administrators
Manages physical infrastructure

questions.question: Which organization defined the NFV framework?
options: IEEE
IETF
ETSI
ITU

questions.question: What is the "service abstraction layer" in SDN controllers?
options: A component for managing hardware resources
A layer for integrating different routing protocols
A middle layer between northbound and southbound interfaces
A tool for visualizing network topology

questions.question: In OpenFlow, what happens when no match is found in the flow table?
options: The packet is dropped
The packet is sent to the controller
The packet is forwarded to all ports
The packet is ignored

questions.question: What is a primary challenge in multi-domain NFV orchestration?
options: Lack of scalability in VNFs
Managing interoperability across different NFV frameworks
High costs of physical hardware
Difficulty in deploying hypervisors

questions.question: What is "proactive configuration" in OpenFlow?
options: Rules are added reactively as packets arrive
Flow entries are pre-populated to avoid delays
Only wildcard flow entries are used
Flow tables are automatically synchronized

questions.question: What is "eventual consistency" in SDN controllers?
options: All changes are immediately reflected in the network
Updates propagate over time, ensuring consistency eventually
All flow rules are applied proactively
The controller ensures data consistency at the packet level

questions.question: Software Defined Networking (SDN) separates:
options: Control plane from data plane.
MAC from IP.
CPU from RAM.
VLAN from LAN.

questions.question: The SDN controller typically:
options: Maintains a (logically) centralized view and programs devices.
Forwards packets at line rate.
Replaces all switches physically.
Is a Layer-2 hub.

questions.question: An advantage of SDN is:
options: Network programmability and flexibility.
More collisions in Ethernet.
Removing routing tables.
Disabling policies.

questions.question: OpenFlow is used to:
options: Install forwarding rules in switches.
Encrypt application payloads.
Assign IP addresses via DHCP.
Carry BGP updates.

questions.question: A scalability issue of centralized control can be:
options: Controller overload or bottlenecks.
ARP cannot work.
Ethernet cannot use full-duplex.
IPv6 cannot be used.

questions.question: In SDN, the data plane is responsible for:
options: Forwarding packets based on rules.
Computing global routing policy.
Managing user accounts.
Creating container images.
